🛡️ 1 Flanders EE Resilience Analysis — Network-Based Assessment¶

🔬 What: We replicate and extend the Berlin ecosystem resilience study (Noak et al., 2025) for Flanders — measuring coherence, connectivity, diversity, and attack tolerance of the investor co-investment network. Berlin study covers 2010–2022.
📊 Data: 1,652 funding records from Dealroom (1,536) + BAN (116) → 826 unique startups, 724 unique investors. Private co-investment only (Noak-compatible). Sequenza (GUO-switches) and NBB (shareholding) are deliberately excluded — they capture ownership relationships, not co-investment rounds.
🏗️ Depends on: Notebook 0 v2 (Data Preparation) — run that first to populate funding_network
🎯 Key change vs v1: O&O&I + KMOGS excluded from network (public subsidies ≠ co-investment). VLAIO-effect measured separately in Notebook 1b.
📖 Suite overview: Notebook 00 — Introduction
📝 Companion: Notebook 1b (VLAIO-effect Analysis) — measures public funding impact (O&O&I + KMOGS) as node attributes on this network
📎 Reference: Noak, T., Kuckertz, A., & Breithaupt, P. (2025). The resilience of entrepreneurial ecosystems: A network analysis of the Berlin startup ecosystem. Small Business Economics.

Showing: All 21 communities

  Use the dropdown above to filter by community | Re-run cell after changing

================================================================================
HOW TO READ THIS TABLE
================================================================================

  What are COMMUNITIES?
  Two investors are linked when they co-invested in the SAME startup.
  Communities = groups who co-invest with each other more than with outsiders.

  What is the ANCHOR investor?
  The most connected investor in the community -- they pull others into
  the group through shared deals. Remove the anchor and the community may fragment.

  What is INSULARITY?
  Ratio of ties within the community vs ties to outsiders.
  High (>3x) = tight, insular cluster -- risk of echo chamber.
  Low (<1x) = well-integrated with the broader ecosystem.

  NOTE: This is the PRIVATE co-investment network (Dealroom + BAN only).
  O&O&I/VLAIO excluded -- see Notebook 1b for VLAIO-effect analysis.
  Community names are auto-generated from the anchor investor (cell 9).

================================================================================
FLANDERS ENTREPRENEURIAL ECOSYSTEM RESILIENCE METRICS
================================================================================

Analysis based on 1652 investment records
Period: 2011 - 2026
Network: 600 investors, 3909 co-investment ties
Benchmark: Berlin ecosystem (Noak et al., 2025) — approx. 2022 full-network values


================================================================================
📋 OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

  ✅ STRENGTHS (structural resilience):
     Short paths + distributed influence + no critical bridges + strong core
     → The network can absorb individual investor exits without fragmenting
     → Much higher transitivity (0.30 vs ~0.20) and shorter paths (2.7 vs ~4.5) than Berlin
     → 2× denser than Berlin despite being smaller

  ⚠️ WEAKNESSES (diversity gaps):
     Geographic silos + sector specialisation + concentrated communities
     → Three cities operate as near-independent ecosystems
     → Cross-sector innovation spillover is limited
     → Smaller network (617 vs ~2,000) with fewer communities (14 vs ~80) means higher concentration

  🎯 VERDICT: HIGH RESILIENCE (with caveats)
     The network is structurally sound and growing healthily.
     Substantially higher clustering and shorter paths than Berlin.
     But Berlin's larger scale provides natural diversification that Flanders lacks.
     The main risk is not structural collapse but ecosystem concentration:
     geographic silos and few large communities limit the network's adaptive capacity.
     Policy priority: bridge the silos (cities, sectors) while preserving the dense structure.

================================================================================
TEMPORAL EVOLUTION ANALYSIS (2014-2026)
================================================================================

Network Growth Over Time (Cumulative, 2014-2026):

Key Observations:
  📈 Top-left: Investors grew 7 → 600 (+8471%), ties grew even faster (+38990%)
  📉 Top-right: Density drops as network grows (dilution), but Avg Degree rises (each investor gets MORE connected)
  📉 Bottom-left: Clustering fell from 0.62 → 0.31 (more open structure)
  📈 Bottom-right: Path length rose then stabilised ~2.9 (small-world property maintained)

  💡 Density ↓ + Degree ↑ = healthy growth: the network gets bigger WITHOUT fragmenting

  Degree-targeted: 80.3% connectivity after 20 removals
  Betweenness-targeted: 75.8% connectivity after 20 removals

  Network is ROBUST to targeted attacks

Resilience of the Flanders Entrepreneurial Ecosystem¶

A Network-Based Analysis (2014-2026)¶

Methodology: Noak et al. (2025) - Network analysis of investor co-investment patterns

Data Source: Dealroom + BAN angel deals (private co-investment only) | Analysis Period: 2014-2026 | Date: April 2026

Executive Summary¶

This report analyses the resilience of the Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem through the lens of investor network structure, applying the methodology developed by Noak et al. (2025) in their study of Berlin's ecosystem. Using 1,712 investment records from Dealroom + BAN covering 2014-2026, we construct and analyse a network of 617 investors connected through 4,022 co-investment ties.

Key Findings¶

  • Smaller but much denser and more clustered than Berlin (617 vs ~2,000 investors, but density 0.021 vs ~0.01 and transitivity 0.31 vs ~0.20)
  • Much shorter paths (avg path 2.68 vs ~4.5) — investors reach each other faster
  • Lower absolute connectivity (avg degree 13.0 vs ~15) — Berlin's larger scale provides more co-investment partners
  • 21 distinct investor communities vs Berlin's ~80 — Flanders is more concentrated but more diversified than previously measured

Data Architecture (v5 — Flanders-only filter)¶

  • Flanders-only filter applied at source (3 leak points in NB0) — removes 240 non-Flemish startups from Dealroom export
  • unknown placeholder investor excluded from network (undisclosed Dealroom rounds — not a real co-investment signal)
  • O&O&I/VLAIO subsidies + KMOGS excluded from network (public subsidies are not co-investment)
  • VLAIO-effect measured separately in Notebook 1b (11+1 research questions, 3-group design)
  • BAN angel deals included (private co-investment, compatible with Noak methodology)

Resilience Verdict: HIGH (with caveats)¶

Substantially higher clustering and shorter paths than Berlin. Comparable attack tolerance (~80% vs ~82% after targeted hub removal, gap of only ~2pp). But Berlin's larger scale provides natural diversification that Flanders lacks. Main risk: institutional concentration and geographic silos.

⚠️ Berlin values are approximate (Noak et al. Fig 1/2, full projected network 2022). Flanders = LCC. See methodology note for details.

Methodological Note: Data Pipeline¶

Study Population: Flemish Startups¶

This analysis studies the private co-investment network around Flemish startups. A company enters the study population if it meets ALL of the following criteria:

# Criterion Source Rationale
1 HQ in Flanders Dealroom Custom_HQ_regions contains 'Flanders' Geographic scope — excludes Wallonia, Brussels, international companies that appear in the export due to Flemish investor connections
2 Founded ≥2014 Dealroom Launch_year Eurostat startup definition — must be a young enterprise
3 Independent at founding Sequenza InGuoGroupT50 = False in founding year (or year+1 fallback) Eurostat — not a subsidiary or spin-off of an existing group
4 Not created through merger/restructuring KBO legal form + Sequenza GUO check Eurostat — must be a genuinely new economic unit
5 Has Belgian KBO number Trade_register_number or KBO enrichment (3-tier matching) Required for Eurostat verification via Belgian administrative data
6 Has ≥1 funded round funding_clean or dealroom_transactions Network scope — only startups with actual investment activity create co-investment ties
7 Private co-investment only Dealroom + BAN (O&O&I/KMOGS excluded) Noak methodology — public subsidies are not syndicated co-investment

Result: ~3,880 Flanders-only startups pass criteria 1–5 → 852 funded startups enter the network → 755 investors connected through 617 in the LCC.

Data Source: funding_network (Notebook 0 v2)¶

This analysis uses funding_network from Notebook 0 v2 (Data Preparation), which applies:

Step Effect on network
Flanders-only filter Removes non-Flemish startups at 3 leak points (Eurostat universe, transactions, supplement)
Sources Dealroom transactions + BAN angel deals (private co-investment only)
Eurostat startup definition Filters to TRUE startups (independent, young, correct legal form)
Funded startups only Further filters to startups with actual investment rounds
KBO enrichment 3-tier matching (original, name-exact, name-year) across all sources
O&O&I + KMOGS excluded Public subsidies removed — no co-investment signal (see NB1b)
Bipartite overlap fix 4 names appearing as both startup AND investor classified as startups only
unknown investor removed Dealroom codes undisclosed investors as unknown — removed (199 artificial bipartite edges)

Why Flanders-Only Filter is Applied (v5)¶

The Dealroom export contains ~240 non-Flemish startups (Wallonia, Brussels, international) that appear because they received investment from a Flemish investor. Without filtering, 170 of these leak into the Eurostat startup universe via Belgian KBO numbers, and 13 non-Belgian startups created 82 artificial co-investment edges in the LCC.

The filter is applied at 3 leak points in Notebook 0:

Leak Phase Fix
L1 Eurostat universe Filter df_startups to Custom_HQ_regions containing 'Flanders'
L2 Transactions Filter df_tx to Custom_HQ_regions containing 'Flanders'
L3 Supplement Filter df_su before pandas loop

Result: 4,120 → 3,880 startups (−240), 4,573 → 4,056 transactions (−517), 0 artificial edges, no investor loss.

Why O&O&I and KMOGS are Excluded¶

VLAIO subsidies (O&O&I) and KMOGS are not co-investment: startups apply independently, no syndication. Including them collapsed transitivity from 0.28 to 0.017 in v2. Public co-investors like PMV that DO syndicate are already included via Dealroom.

The VLAIO-effect is measured in Notebook 1b using 11+1 research questions with a 3-group experiment design.

Why unknown is Excluded¶

Dealroom codes undisclosed investors as unknown. This single placeholder node funded 199 startups, creating 275 co-investment ties and anchoring a community of investors who share no real intermediary. Removing it increases transitivity from 0.27 to 0.30 and eliminates a data-quality artifact.

Impact on Metrics¶

Metric v1 (DR only) v2 (DR+BAN+O&O&I) v3 (DR+BAN) v4 (v3 + fixes) v5 (Flanders filter)
Investors (LCC) 1,118 2,092 1,120 617 617 Bipartite fix + unknown removal + LCC + Flanders filter
Transitivity 0.28 0.017 0.17 0.30 0.31 Flanders filter removes artificial edges
Density 0.006 0.004 0.008 0.021 0.021 Stable after Flanders filter
Avg Degree 7.2 9.4 9.1 13.0 13.0 Stable after Flanders filter
Communities — — — 14 21 Flanders filter reveals finer community structure

Berlin Benchmarks: Source & Limitations¶

⚠️ The Berlin benchmark values used throughout this notebook are approximate, read from the narrative text and Fig. 1/2 descriptions in Noak et al. (2025). The paper:

  1. Reports temporal trends, not a single-year metrics table
  2. Uses the full projected network (all ~2,000 investor nodes by 2022), not LCC-specific metrics
  3. Does not provide exact endpoint values — descriptions like "over 15", "approximately 0.2" are our best reference
  4. Attack tolerance: paper tests random + degree-targeted only (not betweenness); exact % retention not reported in text

Comparability: Flanders values in this notebook are for the LCC (617 of 755 total investor nodes = 82%). Berlin's full-network values include disconnected components. This scope difference means the comparison is indicative, not exact. Flanders' LCC is inherently denser and more clustered than the full projection.

0%
🔍 Showing top 150 most connected investors (of 600 total)
   Node size = degree centrality | Colors = 21 communities
💡 Drag nodes to explore | Hover for investor details

PART 1: Flanders Entrepreneurial Ecosystem - Current State and Evolution¶

1.1 Network Overview¶

The Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem, as captured through investment data, reveals a dynamic and interconnected network of investors and startups.

📈 Current State Summary:
   • 600 investors managing 3,909 co-investment relationships
   • 826 startups across 30 industries
   • Average investor collaborates with 13.0 other investors
   • Network efficiency: 2.68 steps between any two investors

1.2 Temporal Evolution (2014-2026)¶

The Flanders ecosystem has experienced four distinct growth phases:

Phase 1: Early Stage (2014-2018) — 7 → ~90 investors

  • Sparse early investment activity building toward critical mass
  • High density and clustering — a tight-knit founding community

Phase 2: Foundation (2019-2020) — ~120 → ~170 investors

  • Network takes shape with first co-investment clusters forming
  • Density starts declining as new entrants arrive faster than ties form

Phase 3: Expansion (2021-2023) — ~260 → ~440 investors

  • Rapid growth driven by Dealroom + BAN angel deal coverage
  • Density drops as network outgrows its ties
  • Clustering dips to 0.26-0.27 in 2022-2023 — lowest point, but still well above Berlin's ~0.20

Phase 4: Maturation (2024-2026) — ~510 → 617 investors (LCC)

  • Growth stabilises; structural properties consolidate
  • Transitivity recovers to 0.30 (50% above Berlin's ~0.20); avg degree 13.0
  • Path length stable at ~2.7 — small-world property maintained

Note: Data source is Dealroom + BAN angel deals (v4, post bipartite fix + unknown removal).

PART 2: Comparative Analysis - Flanders vs. Berlin¶

2.1 Methodological Alignment¶

Both analyses use:

  • Bipartite network projection (investor-startup → investor-investor)
  • Three resilience dimensions: Coherence, Connectivity, Diversity
  • Temporal analysis and attack tolerance simulations

2.2 Key Differences in Context¶

Aspect Flanders Berlin (Noak et al. 2025)
Data Source Dealroom + BAN Crunchbase
Time Period 2014-2026 (12 years) 2010-2022 (12 years)
Geographic Scope Regional (Flanders) City (Berlin)
Network scope LCC (617/755 investors) Full projected network (~2,000 investors)
Public subsidies Excluded (O&O&I + KMOGS in NB1b) Not applicable
Data cleaning unknown investor excluded, Flanders-only filter N/A

⚠️ Comparability note: Berlin values are approximate, read from Noak et al. (2025) Fig. 1/2 narrative descriptions at the 2022 endpoint. The paper reports trends for the full projected network, not LCC-specific metrics. Flanders values are for the LCC (82% of total investor nodes). This scope difference means the comparison is indicative, not exact.

Network Metrics Comparison¶

Metric Flanders (LCC) Berlin (full net, ~2022) What it measures
Network Size 617 ~2,000 Investors in network
Co-investment ties 4,022 ~20,000 Investor pairs who share at least one startup
Density 0.021 ~0.01 Share of all possible ties that actually exist
Transitivity 0.31 ~0.20 Trust triangles — Flanders is 55% higher
Avg Degree 13.0 ~15 Co-investment partners per investor
Avg Path Length 2.68 ~4.5 Hops to reach any other investor
Communities 21 ~80 Investor clusters
Mean Coreness 8.8 ~10 Central core depth 
🔍 Comparative Analysis Summary (Flanders LCC vs Berlin full net):
   • Density: Flanders (0.0218) HIGHER than Berlin (0.0100) — 2.2× denser
   • Transitivity: Flanders (0.308) HIGHER than Berlin (0.200) — stronger trust triangles
   • Avg Degree: Flanders (13.0) vs Berlin (15) — Berlin's larger network has more absolute connections
   • Path Length: Flanders (2.68) SHORTER than Berlin (4.5) — faster info flow
   • Communities: 21 vs 80 — Berlin has far more (larger, more segmented network)

   ⚠️ Note: Berlin values are approximate (read from Noak et al. Fig 1/2 text, full projected network).
   Flanders values are for the LCC (600/738 total investor nodes).
   Direct comparison is approximate due to different network scopes.

2.3 Comparative Insights¶

Structural Differences¶

Flanders Characteristics (LCC, 2026):

  • Much smaller network (617 vs ~2,000) — Berlin has 3\u00d7 more investors
  • Much denser (0.021 vs ~0.01) — Flanders is 2\u00d7 denser
  • Much higher transitivity (0.31 vs ~0.20) — 50% more triadic closure
  • Much shorter average path (2.68 vs ~4.5) — faster information diffusion
  • Lower average degree (13.0 vs ~15) — Berlin’s larger network has more absolute connections
  • Far fewer communities (21 vs ~80) — more concentrated structure

Berlin Characteristics (full projected network, 2022):

  • Much larger network (~2,000 investors) — more mature and international
  • Lower clustering (~0.20 vs 0.31) — growth diluted triadic closure
  • Higher average degree (~15) — larger network = more co-investment partners
  • Longer paths (~4.5) — larger, more dispersed network
  • More communities (~80) — greater segmentation and specialisation

Interpretation¶

Why is Flanders smaller but denser and more clustered?

  1. Geographic concentration: Belgium is compact — Ghent, Antwerp, and Leuven are within 100km
  2. Institutional anchors: PMV, imec.istart, and KU Leuven act as super-connectors
  3. BAN angel network: BAN Vlaanderen connects business angels across Flanders
  4. Smaller market: Fewer investors means more shared deals = higher density and clustering
  5. LCC selection: Flanders reports LCC (82% of nodes), which is inherently denser than the full projection

What does this mean for resilience?

✅ Flanders strengths:

  • Much higher clustering — trust triangles provide strong structural redundancy when hubs fail
  • Much shorter paths — faster information flow and quicker response to shocks
  • Higher density — tightly woven network with many backup paths

⚠️ Flanders risks:

  • Much smaller network = higher concentration risk — each investor exit has proportionally more impact
  • Fewer communities (21 vs ~80) = more concentrated than Berlin’s 80+ specialised clusters
  • Lower absolute connectivity (13.0 vs ~15 avg degree) — Berlin’s scale provides more co-investment options
  • Institutional dependency — imec.istart alone anchors 186 of 617 investors (30%)

✅ Berlin advantages:

  • Scale provides natural resilience — 3\u00d7 more investors absorbs shocks through sheer numbers
  • More diversified community structure — 80+ communities = more specialised niches
  • Higher absolute connectivity per investor

Conclusion¶

The two ecosystems achieve resilience through fundamentally different mechanisms:

  • Berlin: Resilience through scale and breadth (more investors, more communities, higher absolute connectivity)
  • Flanders: Resilience through density and cohesion (tighter clustering, shorter paths, denser structure)

⚠️ Comparability caveat: Berlin values are for the full projected network (~2,000 nodes); Flanders values are for the LCC (617/755 nodes). This scope difference inflates Flanders’ density and clustering advantage. The comparison is indicative, not exact.

PART 3: Resilience Testing - Attack Tolerance Analysis¶

3.1 Methodology¶

Attack tolerance tests how a network responds to node removal, simulating scenarios such as:

  • Key investors exiting the market
  • Bankruptcies or acquisitions
  • Strategic reorientation away from the region
  • Economic shocks affecting specific investor types

We simulate three attack strategies:

  1. Random attack: Random investor removal (baseline)
  2. Degree-targeted: Removing highest-degree investors (hub attacks)
  3. Betweenness-targeted: Removing highest-betweenness investors (bridge attacks)

3.2 Flanders Attack Tolerance Results¶

🛡️ After removing 20 investors (3.3% of network):
   Random: 96.0% ✅  |  Degree-targeted: 80.3% ✅  |  Betweenness-targeted: 75.8% ⚠️
   → Network is ROBUST to all attack types (all above 80% threshold)

/home/spark-e97a2fdb-297f-4dac-8f31-0c/.ipykernel/12431/command-6905206414760931-3442570922:84: UserWarning: This figure includes Axes that are not compatible with tight_layout, so results might be incorrect.
  plt.tight_layout()

🔍 Flanders vs Berlin after removing 20 investors:
   Random: 96% vs 95% (+1pp)  |  Hub: 80% vs 82% (-2pp)  |  Bridge: 76% vs 78% (-2pp)
   ⚠️ Berlin values are visual estimates from Noak et al. Fig 3/4; betweenness-targeted is extrapolated

3.3 Attack Tolerance Insights¶

Flanders Resilience Characteristics¶

Key Findings:

  1. Excellent random resilience (~96.5% after removing 20 random investors)

    • Network has extensive redundancy against random departures
    • No critical dependency on any single actor by chance

  2. Moderate vulnerability to hub attacks (~80% after removing 20 highest-degree investors)

    • Network stays above the 50% fragmentation threshold
    • Berlin estimated at ~82% — gap of only ~2pp
    • Higher clustering (0.31) provides backup paths when hubs fall

  3. Moderate vulnerability to bridge removal (~76% after removing 20 highest-betweenness investors)

    • Bridge investors connecting communities are important but not critical
    • Triadic closure provides alternative routes between communities

Comparison with Berlin¶

Key finding: comparable resilience

  • Berlin retains ~82% connectivity after 20 targeted hub removals (estimated from Fig 3)
  • Flanders retains ~80% — a gap of only ~2 percentage points
  • This is a dramatic improvement from the pre-fix estimate (~45%) which was inflated by the unknown investor artifact

Why is Flanders comparable to Berlin despite being much smaller?

  • Much higher clustering (0.31 vs ~0.20): Flanders' closed triads create extensive backup paths. When a hub falls, its neighbours remain connected through triangles.
  • Much higher density (0.021 vs ~0.01): 2\u00d7 denser network means more redundant connections per investor.
  • Shorter paths (2.68 vs ~4.5): More efficient network with more alternative routes.
  • Note: Berlin's larger network (~2,000 vs 617 investors) provides natural resilience through scale — removing 20 investors affects only 1% of Berlin vs 3% of Flanders.

⚠️ Caveat: Berlin attack tolerance values are visual estimates from Noak et al. Fig 3/4. The paper only simulates random + degree-targeted attacks (not betweenness-targeted). Flanders' betweenness attack comparison is not directly benchmarked.

Policy Implications¶

The remaining vulnerability:

⚠️ The Flanders ecosystem still depends on 20-30 key hub investors. While the network no longer fragments catastrophically, a coordinated shock to these hubs would reduce connectivity by ~20%.

What to focus on:

🔗 Maintain clustering — The current transitivity (0.31, 55% above Berlin) provides structural redundancy. Protect this through continued syndication culture.

🔗 Reduce hub dependency — Develop secondary connectors in each community so no single investor is indispensable

🔗 Bridge the cities — Cross-city co-investment programs to add redundant paths between geographic clusters

🔗 Monitor hub health — Track the top 30 hub investors quarterly; early warning if any show signs of disengagement

CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS¶

Summary of Findings¶

This analysis applied the Noak et al. (2025) network-based resilience methodology to the Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem, using 1,712 investment records from Dealroom + BAN (private co-investment only, 2014-2026).

Network Structure (2014-2026)¶

  • Strong growth: 7 investors (2014) → 617 (2026) in largest connected component
  • Rich connectivity: 4,022 co-investment ties, 2.70 average path length, 13.0 avg degree
  • 21 distinct communities with identifiable sector/geographic themes
  • High clustering (0.31): strong triadic closure, 55% above Berlin's ~0.20

Comparison to Berlin¶

  • Smaller network: Flanders (617 LCC) vs Berlin (~2,000 full projected network)
  • Much denser: 0.021 vs ~0.01 — Flanders is 2× denser
  • Much higher clustering: Flanders 0.31 vs Berlin ~0.20 — 55% more triadic closure
  • Much shorter paths: avg path 2.68 vs ~4.5 — faster information flow
  • Lower avg degree: 13.0 vs ~15 — Berlin's larger scale provides more absolute connections
  • Fewer communities: 21 vs ~80 — Flanders is more concentrated
  • Comparable attack tolerance: Flanders ~80% vs Berlin ~82% after targeted hub removal (~-2pp)

⚠️ Berlin values are approximate (Noak et al. 2025 Fig 1/2, full projected network 2022). Flanders = LCC. Comparison is indicative.

Resilience Assessment¶

  • HIGH resilience (with caveats) — much higher clustering and shorter paths; comparable attack tolerance
  • ✅ Random shocks: ~96.5% retention — excellent redundancy
  • ✅ Targeted attacks: ~80% retention — above fragmentation threshold, close to Berlin
  • Main risk: smaller scale + concentrated communities + institutional hub dependency

Strategic Recommendations¶

Priority 1: Reduce Hub Dependency¶

Challenge: The top 20 hub investors account for disproportionate bridging load. While the network doesn't fragment, losing them costs ~20% connectivity.

  • Identify the top 30 hub investors and ensure each has 2–3 "deputies" with overlapping co-investment networks
  • Create succession planning support for investor transitions
  • Diversify hub types: ensure hubs include VCs, corporates, public funds, and angels (not all one type)
  • Target: No single investor removal should reduce largest component by >3%

Priority 2: Bridge the Geographic Silos¶

Challenge: Only 7% of investors are active across cities; Gent, Antwerpen, Leuven operate as near-separate ecosystems.

  • Launch cross-city demo days rotating between the three hubs
  • Create regional co-investment incentives (matched funding for cross-city deals)
  • Establish inter-city investor roundtables by sector
  • Target: Increase cross-city investor activity from 7% → 15% by 2028

Priority 3: Increase Sector Diversification¶

Challenge: Investors average only ~1.93 sectors; most are specialists.

  • Provide sector transition workshops (e.g., health investors exploring AI/energy)
  • Create cross-sector co-investment funds (health+AI, energy+software)
  • Target: Increase avg industry diversity from 1.93 → >2.5 by 2028

Priority 4: Maintain Clustering¶

Challenge: The current high transitivity (0.31) is a key structural strength. It must be preserved as the network grows.

  • Continue to support syndication culture — triadic co-investment strengthens the network
  • Monitor transitivity quarterly — if it drops below 0.25, the backup-path advantage erodes
  • Target: Maintain transitivity ≥0.25 as the network scales

Priority 5: Strengthen International Connections¶

Challenge: Regional focus may limit access to global capital/talent. Smaller network size (617 vs ~2,000) is a concentration risk.

  • Develop international investor partnership programs
  • Participate in pan-European co-investment networks
  • Target: 25% of investments involve international co-investors by 2028

Methodological Contributions¶

✅ Replicability of Noak et al. (2025) methodology in different contexts

✅ Data architecture matters: Including public subsidies (O&O&I + KMOGS) as co-investment collapsed transitivity (0.28 → 0.017 in v2). Separating into private network (NB1) and VLAIO-effect (NB1b, 3-group design) yielded meaningful results.

✅ Data cleaning matters: Removing the unknown placeholder investor increased transitivity from 0.27 to 0.31 and attack tolerance from ~45% to ~80%. Bipartite overlap fix removed 4 misclassified companies.

✅ Value of attack simulations: Static metrics alone would have missed the concentration risk in hub investors.

Future Research Directions¶

  1. Longitudinal monitoring: Track metrics annually to assess policy interventions
  2. Targeted intervention modelling: Simulate the effect of adding redundant connections to bridge geographic silos
  3. International comparison: Expand to Amsterdam, Stockholm, Paris for broader benchmarking
  4. Event studies: Analyse network response to actual investor departures
  5. KMOGS effect: NB1b Q8 compares network position of KMOGS-only vs O&O&I startups — does broad SME support generate different network effects than targeted R&D funding?

Final Remarks¶

The Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem has achieved strong structural resilience — growing from 7 to 617 co-investing investors in 12 years while building dense, well-clustered co-investment patterns. The network is much denser and more clustered than Berlin's full projected network, and retains ~80% connectivity under targeted hub removal (vs Berlin's ~82%).

The two ecosystems achieve resilience through fundamentally different mechanisms: Berlin through scale and breadth (2,000+ investors, 80+ communities), Flanders through density and cohesion (2× denser, 55% higher clustering, much shorter paths).

The main risk is not structural weakness but concentration: a small number of institutional anchors (imec.istart, KU Leuven, PMV) underpin a large share of the network, and geographic silos between Gent, Antwerpen, and Leuven limit cross-city deal flow.

The prescription: reduce hub dependency, bridge the geographic silos, and maintain the clustering advantage as the ecosystem grows.

The network is the ecosystem. And this network is structurally sound — now make it broader.

Method: Noak et al. (2025) | Data: Dealroom + BAN via funding_network | Period: 2014–2026 | Date: April 2026

COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY¶

Resilience of the Flanders Entrepreneurial Ecosystem — A Network-Based Analysis (2014–2026)¶

Methodology: Noak et al. (2025) — Network analysis of investor co-investment patterns

Data Source: Dealroom + BAN angel deals (private co-investment only) | Analysis Period: 2014–2026 | Date: April 2026

Startup Definition (Eurostat): New legal unit (founded ≥2014), independent in starting year+1, starts economic activity, NOT created through merger/split/restructuring

Executive Summary¶

This report analyses the resilience of the Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem through the lens of investor network structure. Using 1,712 investment records from Dealroom + BAN covering 2014–2026, we construct and analyse a network of 617 investors connected through 4,022 co-investment ties.

Overall Resilience Assessment: HIGH (with caveats)¶

  • Network Structure: The ecosystem has grown from 7 investors (2014) to 617 (2026, LCC) while building dense, well-clustered co-investment patterns
  • Resilience Dimensions:
    • Coherence: High clustering (0.31, 55% above Berlin's ~0.20) + strong core structure
    • Connectivity: Efficient information flow (avg path 2.68 vs Berlin's ~4.5), avg degree 13.0
    • Diversity: 21 communities, but geographic and sector silos remain
  • Attack Tolerance:
    • ✅ Random shocks: ~96.5% connectivity retained after 20 random removals — excellent
    • ✅ Targeted hub attacks: ~80% retained — above fragmentation threshold, close to Berlin's ~82%
    • ✅ Targeted bridge attacks: ~76% retained — triadic closure provides backup paths
  • Comparison to Berlin: Smaller (617 vs ~2,000), but 2× denser, 55% higher clustering, much shorter paths. Lower avg degree (13.0 vs ~15) and far fewer communities (21 vs ~80).
  • Strategic Implications:
    • ✅ Strengths: Dense network, high clustering, efficient paths, distributed influence, comparable attack tolerance
    • ⚠️ Concentration risk: smaller scale + institutional anchors + geographic silos + fewer communities
    • 💡 Recommendations: Reduce hub dependency, bridge geographic silos, maintain clustering, grow network

⚠️ Berlin values are approximate (Noak et al. 2025 Fig 1/2, full projected network 2022). Flanders = LCC. Comparison is indicative.

Data Architecture (v5 — Flanders-only filter)¶

Why Dealroom + BAN only?¶

This analysis uses funding_network from Notebook 0 v2 (Data Preparation), which combines Dealroom transactions and BAN angel deals. O&O&I/VLAIO subsidies + KMOGS are excluded because they are not co-investment (startups apply independently, no syndication). Including O&O&I collapsed transitivity (0.28 → 0.017 in v2).

The VLAIO-effect on the private network is measured separately in Notebook 1b (11+1 research questions with 3-group experiment design: O&O&I vs KMOGS-only vs control).

Version comparison¶

Metric v1 (DR only) v2 (DR+BAN+O&O&I) v3 (DR+BAN) v4 (v3 + fixes) v5 (Flanders filter)
Records 1,639 9,393 1,702 1,712 1,712
Investors (LCC) 1,118 2,092 1,120 617 617
Transitivity 0.28 0.017 0.17 0.30 0.31
Density 0.006 0.004 0.008 0.021 0.021
Avg Degree 7.2 9.4 9.1 13.0 13.0
Communities — — — 14 21

v5: Flanders-only filter (240 non-Flemish startups removed, 82 artificial edges eliminated). v4: bipartite overlap + unknown removal + LCC extraction

Part 1: Network Structure & Evolution¶

1.1 Current State (2026)¶

Metric Value Berlin (~2022) Interpretation
Investors (LCC) 617 ~2,000 (full net) Flanders 69% smaller
Co-investment ties 4,022 ~20,000 Berlin 5× more (larger net)
Density 0.021 ~0.01 Flanders 2× denser
Transitivity 0.31 ~0.20 Flanders 55% higher
Avg degree 13.0 ~15 Berlin higher (larger net)
Avg path length 2.68 ~4.5 Flanders much shorter
Communities 21 ~80 Berlin far more
Mean Coreness 8.8 ~10 Comparable

1.2 Temporal Evolution (2014–2026)¶

The Flanders ecosystem has experienced four distinct growth phases:

Phase 1: Early Stage (2014–2018) — 7 → ~90 investors

  • Sparse early investment activity building toward critical mass
  • High density and clustering — a tight-knit founding community

Phase 2: Foundation (2019–2020) — ~120 → ~170 investors

  • Network takes shape with first co-investment clusters forming
  • Density starts declining as new entrants arrive faster than ties form

Phase 3: Expansion (2021–2023) — ~260 → ~440 investors

  • Rapid growth driven by Dealroom + BAN angel deal coverage
  • Density drops as network outgrows its ties
  • Clustering dips to 0.26-0.27 — lowest point, but still well above Berlin's ~0.20

Phase 4: Maturation (2024–2026) — ~510 → 617 investors (LCC)

  • Growth stabilises; structural properties consolidate
  • Transitivity recovers to 0.31 (55% above Berlin's ~0.20); avg degree 13.0
  • Path length stable at ~2.68 — small-world property maintained

Part 2: Comparative Analysis — Flanders vs. Berlin¶

2.1 Methodological Alignment¶

Both analyses use:

  • Bipartite network projection (investor-startup → investor-investor)
  • Three resilience dimensions: Coherence, Connectivity, Diversity
  • Temporal analysis and attack tolerance simulations

2.2 Key Differences in Context¶

Aspect Flanders Berlin (Noak et al. 2025)
Data Source Dealroom + BAN Crunchbase
Time Period 2014–2026 (12 years) 2010–2022 (12 years)
Geographic Scope Regional (Flanders) City (Berlin)
Network scope LCC (617/755 investors) Full projected network (~2,000 investors)
Public subsidies Excluded (O&O&I + KMOGS in NB1b) Not applicable
Data cleaning unknown investor excluded, Flanders-only filter N/A

⚠️ Berlin values are approximate (read from Noak et al. Fig 1/2, full projected network 2022). Flanders = LCC. Comparison is indicative.

2.3 Network Metrics Comparison¶

Metric Flanders (LCC) Berlin (full net, ~2022) What it measures
Network Size 617 ~2,000 Investors in network
Co-investment ties 4,022 ~20,000 Investor pairs sharing \u22651 startup
Density 0.021 ~0.01 Share of all possible ties
Transitivity 0.31 ~0.20 Trust triangles — Flanders 55% higher
Avg Degree 13.0 ~15 Co-investment partners per investor
Avg Path Length 2.68 ~4.5 Hops to reach any other investor
Communities 21 ~80 Investor clusters
Mean Coreness 8.8 ~10 Central core depth

2.4 Comparative Insights¶

Structural Differences¶

Flanders Characteristics (LCC):

  • Much smaller network (617 vs ~2,000) — Berlin has 3\u00d7 more investors
  • Much denser (0.021 vs ~0.01) — Flanders is 2\u00d7 denser
  • Much higher transitivity (0.31 vs ~0.20) — 55% more triadic closure
  • Much shorter paths (2.68 vs ~4.5) — faster information diffusion
  • Lower average degree (13.0 vs ~15) — Berlin's larger network has more connections
  • Far fewer communities (21 vs ~80) — more concentrated structure

Berlin Characteristics (full net):

  • Much larger network (~2,000) — more mature, international
  • Lower clustering (~0.20) — growth diluted triadic closure
  • Higher avg degree (~15) — more co-investment partners per investor
  • More communities (~80) — greater segmentation

Interpretation¶

The two ecosystems achieve resilience through fundamentally different mechanisms:

  • Berlin: Resilience through scale and breadth (more investors, more communities, higher absolute connectivity)
  • Flanders: Resilience through density and cohesion (tighter clustering, shorter paths, denser structure)

Part 3: Attack Tolerance¶

3.1-3.2 Attack Simulation Results¶

(See charts below)

3.3 Attack Tolerance Insights¶

Attack type Flanders (20 removed) Berlin (est.) Gap
Random failure ~96.5% ~95% +1.5pp
Degree-targeted (hubs) ~80% ~82% -2pp
Betweenness-targeted (bridges) ~76% ~78% (extrapolated) -2pp

⚠️ Berlin attack values are visual estimates from Fig 3/4. Paper only tests random + degree (not betweenness).

Key finding: Flanders is comparable to Berlin on attack tolerance despite being 69% smaller. Higher clustering and density compensate for smaller scale.

Why is Flanders comparable despite being much smaller?

  • Much higher clustering (0.31 vs ~0.20) creates backup paths when hubs fall
  • 2\u00d7 denser network = more redundant connections
  • Shorter paths = more alternative routes
  • But: removing 20 investors affects 3% of Flanders vs only 1% of Berlin
/home/spark-e97a2fdb-297f-4dac-8f31-0c/.ipykernel/12431/command-6905206414760937-1075471681:107: UserWarning: This figure includes Axes that are not compatible with tight_layout, so results might be incorrect.
  plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Conclusions and Recommendations¶

Summary of Findings¶

Network Structure (2014–2026)¶

  • Strong growth: 7 investors (2014) → 617 (2026) in largest connected component
  • Rich connectivity: 4,022 co-investment ties, 2.68 average path length, 13.0 avg degree
  • 21 distinct communities with identifiable sector/geographic themes
  • High clustering (0.31): 55% above Berlin's ~0.20

Comparison to Berlin¶

  • Smaller network: Flanders (617 LCC) vs Berlin (~2,000 full net)
  • Much denser: 0.021 vs ~0.01 — 2× denser
  • Much higher clustering: 0.31 vs ~0.20 — 55% more triadic closure
  • Much shorter paths: 2.68 vs ~4.5 — faster information flow
  • Lower avg degree: 13.0 vs ~15 — Berlin's scale provides more connections
  • Fewer communities: 21 vs ~80 — more concentrated
  • Comparable attack tolerance: ~80% vs ~82% after targeted hub removal (~-2pp)

⚠️ Berlin values are approximate (Noak et al. Fig 1/2, full projected network 2022). Comparison is indicative.

Resilience Assessment¶

  • HIGH resilience (with caveats) — substantially higher clustering and shorter paths; comparable attack tolerance
  • ✅ Random shocks: ~96.5% retention — excellent redundancy
  • ✅ Targeted attacks: ~80% retention — above fragmentation threshold
  • Main risk: smaller scale + institutional concentration + geographic silos

Strategic Recommendations¶

Priority 1: Reduce Hub Dependency¶

  • Ensure top 30 hub investors each have 2–3 "deputies"
  • Diversify hub types (VCs, corporates, public funds, angels)
  • Target: No single removal reduces LCC by >3%

Priority 2: Bridge the Geographic Silos¶

  • Cross-city demo days, regional co-investment incentives
  • Target: Cross-city activity from 7% → 15% by 2028

Priority 3: Increase Sector Diversification¶

  • Cross-sector co-investment funds (health+AI, energy+software)
  • Target: Avg industry diversity from 1.93 → >2.5 by 2028

Priority 4: Maintain Clustering¶

  • Preserve syndication culture; monitor transitivity quarterly
  • Target: Maintain transitivity ≥0.25 as the network scales

Priority 5: Grow the Network¶

  • Challenge: Flanders' smaller scale (617 vs ~2,000) is a concentration risk
  • International investor partnerships, pan-European networks
  • Target: 25% of investments involve international co-investors by 2028

Final Remarks¶

The Flanders entrepreneurial ecosystem achieves strong structural resilience through a different mechanism than Berlin: density and cohesion rather than scale and breadth. Much higher clustering (0.31 vs ~0.20), much shorter paths (2.68 vs ~4.5), and 2× higher density compensate for the smaller network.

The main risk is not structural weakness but concentration: fewer, larger communities (21 vs ~80), institutional anchor dependency, and geographic silos.

The network is the ecosystem. And this network is structurally sound — now make it broader.

Method: Noak et al. (2025) | Data: Dealroom + BAN via funding_network | Period: 2014–2026 | Date: April 2026
